entries.position_summary: The country welcomes broad support for risk reduction measures and asserts that such measures complement Article VI disarmament objectives, contributing to the prevention of nuclear war. It frames risk reduction as aligned with the NPT’s disarmament commitments.
entries.quote: Second, we would like to welcome the progress made on key issues. For example, we welcome the strong support of many States Parties for risk reduction. As we have frequently stated, risk reduction complements Article VI goals and contributes to our common interest in preventing nuclear war.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports establishing a Middle East zone free of WMD and delivery systems, contingent on arrangements freely agreed by all regional states. It advocates direct, inclusive dialogue to build confidence and address legitimate security concerns, and is prepared to engage in consensus-based initiatives to implement the 1995 Resolution, noting developments from the UN Conference sessions.
entries.quote: Chair, The United States remains committed to the goal of a Middle East zone free of weapons of mass destruction and delivery systems, based on arrangements freely arrived at by all regional states. We are convinced that the only path to progress is through direct, inclusive dialogue aimed at building confidence and addressing the legitimate security concerns of all parties. We are prepared to engage on initiatives to advance implementation of the 1995 Resolution that have consensus regional support. The United States took note of developments during the first three sessions of the UN Conference on the establishment of such a zone,

entries.position_summary: The country commits to concrete risk reduction measures, especially during heightened tensions, to promote stability and build the confidence necessary for further steps. It frames risk reduction as a prerequisite for broader progress.
entries.quote: It is why we remain committed to advancing concrete risk reduction measures, measures that are especially needed when tensions are high, in order to promote stability and to provide the confidence needed to pursue further steps.

entries.position_summary: The country urges Russia to return to compliance with New START and to engage in talks to manage nuclear risks. It supports negotiating a post-2026 nuclear arms control framework.
entries.quote: It is time for Russia to return to compliance with New START and engage with us to manage nuclear risks and discuss a post-2026 nuclear arms control framework.

entries.position_summary: The country supports arms control engagement, risk reduction measures, and advancing a fissile material cutoff treaty to strengthen the NPT. It also stresses the importance of negative security assurances and calls on all states to uphold the assurances they have given.
entries.quote: Steps such as these – on arms control engagement, on risk reduction, and on Cutoff – can help strengthen the NPT’s role as a positive force for restraint and diplomacy. So too do negative security assurances, and we call on all states to uphold the assurances they have given.

entries.position_summary: The country calls for using the NPT review process to advance nuclear restraint through renewed arms control and concrete risk-reduction measures. It insists on full compliance with IAEA safeguards and supports expanding peaceful nuclear uses globally.
entries.quote: At a time of rising instability and risk, let’s use this review process to advance policies and practices of nuclear restraint, including action on arms control and risk reduction, insisting on full compliance with IAEA safeguards, and facilitating an expansion of peaceful uses on a global scale.

entries.position_summary: The country criticizes China for refusing to engage in bilateral risk-reduction and arms-control discussions, noting Beijing’s suspension of their bilateral consultations. It implies a desire for such engagement that is not being reciprocated by the PRC.
entries.quote: PRC continues to show no interest in engaging bilaterally on risk reduction or arms control, as demonstrated by its suspension of our bilateral consultations.

entries.position_summary: The country urges Russia and China to engage in arms control and risk-reduction discussions. It offered to negotiate a post‑February 2026 arms control framework without preconditions, but states that Russia has refused to participate.
entries.quote: Over the past two years, we have pressed Russia and the PRC to engage on arms control and risk reduction, including by urging Russia to work with us to develop a post-February 2026 arms control framework, which it has refused to do. We offered to do so without preconditions.

entries.position_summary: The United States is proactively convening expert discussions among the five nuclear-weapon States focused on nuclear doctrines and risk reduction. It seeks to enhance transparency and build confidence despite acknowledged difficulties.
entries.quote: That is why the United States has convened multiple expert discussions on nuclear doctrines and risk reduction among the five nuclear-weapon States – despite the obvious difficulties to doing so.

entries.position_summary: The United States reaffirms its commitment to disarmament consistent with the Thirteen Steps and Article VI, stating readiness to pursue critical arms control measures. It seeks to reduce nuclear risks through bilateral engagement with Russia and China and via the P5 process, despite their current unwillingness to engage.
entries.quote: The “Thirteen Steps” clearly identified the need for all Nuclear Weapon States to engage in the disarmament process. It is because of the seriousness with which we take our disarmament-related commitments that President Biden made clear at the UN last September that “no matter what else is happening in the world, the United States is ready to pursue critical arms control measures.” It's also why we will continue to seek to reduce nuclear risks with Russia and the PRC bilaterally and through the P5 process, despite their unwillingness to engage.

entries.position_summary: The United States emphasizes transparency as a priority, using it to support arms control efforts and to build confidence among NPT parties. It views openness as a means to strengthen mutual trust and the effectiveness of arms control.
entries.quote: Finally, the United States prioritizes transparency, whether as a tool of arms control or to build confidence among NPT parties.

entries.position_summary: The country pledges negative security assurances to all NPT non-nuclear-weapon states in compliance with their obligations. It also reaffirms that none of its nuclear weapons are targeted at any state, a de-targeting measure that serves as a confidence-building step. These statements, made via its Nuclear Posture Review, reflect transparency about its nuclear policy.
entries.quote: As stated in our Nuclear Posture Review, we also provide negative security assurances to all NPT non-nuclear-weapon states that are in compliance with their nuclear nonproliferation obligations. Further, we have repeatedly reaffirmed that none of our nuclear weapons are targeted at any other state.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports robust transparency measures, including those within the NPT review process, to reduce misunderstandings and build confidence. It emphasizes aligning words and actions and adhering to prior NPT review conference consensus decisions.
entries.quote: I also wish to reemphasize that it is the United States’ view that transparency measures, including those taken through this review process, can play a critical role in reducing misunderstandings and reassuring others that a state’s words and actions are in alignment, including as it pertains to prior consensus decisions of NPT review conferences.

entries.position_summary: The statement condemns Russia for violating its security assurances to Ukraine under the Budapest Memorandum and criticizes its purported suspension of New START and irresponsible nuclear rhetoric. It argues these actions increase nuclear risks and the chances of miscalculation, contrasting them with P3 efforts to engage responsibly on negative security assurances.
entries.quote: Unfortunately, in contrast to P3 efforts to engage responsibly on negative security assurances, the Russian Federation has disregarded the commitments and assurances it made in the Budapest Memorandum to respect the independence, sovereignty, and territorial integrity of Ukraine. Russia’s legally invalid, purported suspension of the New START Treaty and irresponsible rhetoric regarding nuclear weapons not only increase nuclear risks, but also heighten the possibility of miscalculation.

entries.position_summary: The United States emphasizes transparency through its NPR and stockpile disclosures, reports steep unilateral reductions including in non-strategic weapons, and highlights measures lowering operational status and the role of nuclear weapons in its strategy. It encourages China and Russia to adopt similar transparency. This is presented as part of fulfilling disarmament commitments and advancing risk reduction.
entries.quote: well as in the public release of information in our Nuclear Posture Review and pertaining to our nuclear weapons stockpile. The U.S. national report to the 2022 RevCon also provided detailed information on other relevant topics, including steep U.S. reductions in non-strategic nuclear weapons and other unilateral cuts beyond treaty-based reductions over the past three decades, significant measures taken to reduce operational status of nuclear weapons systems, and the reduced role of nuclear weapons in U.S. national security strategy, including with respect to the 2022 NPR’s Declaratory Policy. We encourage the PRC and Russia to provide similar transparency.

entries.position_summary: The United States underscores the importance of the NPT and commits to collaborating with other states to reduce and eliminate nuclear threats. It pledges to pursue risk reduction and arms control efforts that enhance the security of the U.S., its allies, and partners.
entries.quote: In our main Cluster I statement, the United States reaffirmed its commitment to enhancing global security and working with other states towards the mutual goal of reducing and eliminating nuclear threats. We stressed the importance of the NPT in that regard. In line with President Trump’s stated objectives, we will pursue risk reduction and arms control efforts that advance U.S., allied, and partner security.

entries.position_summary: The country signals intent to engage on arms control, nuclear risk reduction, and effective verification, prioritizes full compliance with IAEA safeguards, and supports expanding access to peaceful uses of nuclear technology ahead of the next Review Conference.
entries.quote: Ahead of next year’s Review Conference, there is much to be done. Over the next year, we look forward to engaging on ideas for arms control, reducing nuclear risk, and effective verification; on ensuring full compliance with IAEA safeguards; and on expanding access to peaceful uses. As Secretary of State Rubio has repeatedly said, “peace is not a noun, it is a verb.” So, let’s get to work.

entries.position_summary: The United States urges the P5 to pursue multilateral arms control and risk-reduction measures to prevent nuclear war and avoid arms races. As P5 Chair, it convened multiple expert-level meetings in 2023 to advance these efforts.
entries.quote: In parallel, the five nuclear weapons states must continue to engage in multilateral arms control and risk reduction efforts; they have an urgent responsibility to work together to prevent nuclear war and avoid arms races. As the P5 Chair, the United States has held multiple expert level meetings in 2023 to that end.

entries.position_summary: The United States reaffirms its unequivocal commitment to a world without nuclear weapons and to pursuing general and complete disarmament under effective international controls. It underscores the value of bilateral arms control, notes past reductions, and expresses readiness to engage Russia and China on future frameworks and risk reduction, while warning that Russia’s noncompliance jeopardizes prospects for a post-2026 New START successor. It aligns with the P5 view that a nuclear war cannot be won and must never be fought.
entries.quote: the substance of which we support. 2000 Steps Six and Eleven; 2010 Actions Three and Five: Unequivocal Undertaking to Eliminate Nuclear Arsenals and Reaffirmation of Objective for General and Complete Disarmament: On Steps Six and Eleven, the unequivocal U.S. commitment to the peace and security of a world without nuclear weapons cannot be doubted, and consistent with the 2022 P5 Joint Leaders statement, we recognize that a nuclear war cannot be won and must never be fought. The only way to totally eliminate all nuclear risk is through complete, irreversible, global nuclear disarmament. And we remain committed to the Thirteen Step’s call for pursuing in good faith measures toward general and complete disarmament under strict and effective international controls. 2000 Step Seven; 2010 Action Four: START II, START III, and ABM Treaties: On Step Seven, the 2000 Review Conference spoke to the importance of bilateral arms control agreements. Over the past three decades, the United States has made significant reductions in nuclear weapons – both unilaterally and through arms control agreements with the former Soviet Union and then Russia. The arms control environment has clearly evolved since 2000, and specific topics from this step have been overtaken by developments. We have made clear our readiness to engage on future arms control frameworks with Russia and to work to reduce nuclear risks with the PRC. However, Russia’s noncompliance with existing obligations and outright rejection of arms control dialogue casts a shadow over the likelihood of a New START successor after February 2026. Meanwhile, the

entries.position_summary: The country calls on the P5 to adopt concrete risk-reduction steps, including crisis communication channels, formalized ballistic missile launch notifications, and a commitment to maintain a human-in-the-loop for nuclear command and employment. It regrets that two of the five nuclear-weapon states have not engaged substantively with these proposals.
entries.quote: We proposed risk reduction steps to the P5, such as establishing crisis communication channels among the five nuclear-weapon states, formalizing ballistic missile launch notifications, and committing to maintain a “human-in-the- loop” for the command, control, and employment of nuclear weapons. We regret that two of these states have yet to engage substantively on this agenda.

entries.position_summary: The country presses Russia to uphold arms control and pursue risk reduction, rejecting Russia’s purported suspension of New START as legally invalid and condemning its nuclear rhetoric for heightening risks of miscalculation. It also criticizes China’s rapid and opaque nuclear buildup, calling into question Beijing’s transparency and intentions.
entries.quote: We continue to press Russia on arms control and risk reduction despite its legally invalid purported suspension of the New START Treaty and its rhetoric regarding nuclear weapons that not only increase nuclear risks but also heighten the possibility of miscalculation. Meanwhile, China’s rapid and opaque nuclear weapons build-up raises questions about Beijing’s 2

entries.position_summary: The country notes that, despite broad interest in risk reduction and in linking peaceful uses of nuclear technology to the Sustainable Development Goals, the NPT Preparatory Committee has failed to reach consensus on substantive recommendations. This implies support for progress on these topics and concern about the functioning of the review process.
entries.quote: Even when there is a strong convergence of interest on certain topics, such as risk reduction or linking peaceful uses to Sustainable Development Goals, the Preparatory Committee has never been able to reach consensus on substantive recommendations on those or any other topics.

entries.position_summary: The United States praises the NPT’s success and urges States Parties to use the PrepCom to advance the Treaty through arms control, reducing risks of nuclear conflict, ensuring full compliance with IAEA safeguards, and expanding access to peaceful uses of nuclear technology.
entries.quote: Statement by the United States to the NPT Preparatory Committee Statement by Deputy Assistant Secretary of State Gonzalo Suarez, U.S. Department of State, Bureau of International Security and Nonproliferation New York, NY May 9, 2025 Chair, colleagues, In our opening statement last week, the United States praised the remarkable success of this Treaty over the past fifty-five years and reaffirmed that all States Parties continue to benefit greatly from it. We urged all States Parties to use this PrepCom to develop positive ideas that advance the NPT, whether on arms control, reducing the risk of nuclear conflict, ensuring full compliance with IAEA safeguards, or expanding access to peaceful uses.

entries.position_summary: The United States (with France and the UK) emphasizes transparency and responsible practices, declaring excess fissile material and placing it and all civilian nuclear facilities under IAEA safeguards while annually reporting civil plutonium holdings. It frames these steps as risk-reduction measures that prevent miscalculation and support progress on disarmament commitments under the NPT. The statement contrasts this approach with what it describes as the opacity of China and Russia.
entries.quote: On Steps Eight and Ten, the United States has also declared hundreds of tons of fissile material in excess to nuclear weapons needs and has made much of it available for IAEA safeguards during storage and disposition. In stark contrast to the PRC, the United States makes all our civilian nuclear facilities available for IAEA safeguards and reports annually to the IAEA our civil plutonium holdings. 2000 Steps Nine and Twelve; 2010 Actions Nineteen through Twenty-One: Other Steps for NWS, Transparency, and Reporting: On Steps Nine and Twelve, the P3 commitment to transparency is striking in comparison to the opacity shown by the PRC and Russia. The Working Paper submitted to the 2022 RevCon by the United States, France, and the UK on “Principles and responsible practices for Nuclear Weapon States” represents our shared commitment to transparency and to acting responsibly to reduce nuclear risks, prevent unintended miscalculation and escalation, and support progress on our disarmament commitments. This commitment is also reflected in our NPT national report, which lays out in greater detail the actions I am describing, as

entries.position_summary: The United States reaffirms commitment to the NPT and the goal of a world without nuclear weapons, reflecting support for Article VI disarmament. It signals willingness to work with all nations to reduce strategic risks and enhance global security.
entries.quote: Chair, The United States remains fully committed to the NPT and the goal of a world without nuclear weapons. We look forward to working with all nations to reduce strategic risks and enhance global security.

entries.position_summary: The country reaffirms commitment to a world without nuclear weapons while warning that Russia, China, and North Korea are rapidly expanding and diversifying their arsenals. It calls for collective pressure on these states to undertake immediate nuclear risk-reduction measures.
entries.quote: Such a step would not reflect an abandonment of our principles or commitment to pursuing the shared goal of a world without nuclear weapons. But we cannot ignore the current security environment in which Russia, the PRC, and the DPRK are all expanding and diversifying their arsenals at breakneck pace. That is why we need your collective voices in demanding that all these countries engage in nuclear risk reduction efforts without further delay.

entries.position_summary: Expresses concern over the expanding nuclear arsenals and delivery systems of India and Pakistan, and urges all nuclear-armed states to exercise restraint in developing nuclear and missile capabilities.
entries.quote: And finally, Chair, we remain concerned by the growing number of nuclear weapons and delivery systems in India and Pakistan and continue to encourage all states with nuclear weapons to exercise restraint regarding nuclear and missile capabilities.

entries.position_summary: The statement endorses the principle that a nuclear war cannot be won and must never be fought, emphasizing the need to ensure nuclear weapons are never used. It urges other nuclear-weapon states to uphold their 2022 reaffirmation of this risk-reduction principle.
entries.quote: President Trump has also spoken about the enormous power of nuclear weapons and the imperative to ensure there is never a need for such weapons to be used again, echoing President Reagan’s statement that “a nuclear war cannot be won and must never be fought.” We remind all other Nuclear Weapons States that they reaffirmed this principle in 2022.

entries.position_summary: The United States reaffirms commitment to the NPT and the ultimate goal of a world without nuclear weapons. It emphasizes working with all nations to reduce strategic risks and enhance global security in the interim.
entries.quote: Chair, The United States remains fully committed to the NPT and the goal of a world without nuclear weapons. We look forward to working with everyone here and with all nations, pending the achievement of that goal, to reduce strategic risks and enhance global security.

entries.position_summary: The country supports engaging China and Russia to pursue verifiable arms control that reduces nuclear conflict risk. It prioritizes developing and partnering on verification technologies and emphasizes creating a security environment conducive to disarmament, including through risk reduction measures.
entries.quote: President Trump has made clear his readiness to engage China and Russia to reduce the risks of nuclear conflict. We will seek verifiable arms control arrangements that enhance American national security. We have pursued diligently the technology necessary to advance future nuclear disarmament treaties, leading a decade of partnership with states to ensure that future disarmament measures can be effectively verified. We also emphasize the importance of creating a security environment conducive to disarmament, including by using risk reduction measures to avoid inadvertent escalation.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports a Middle East WMD-free zone and related regional initiatives pursued voluntarily and by mutual agreement among all regional states, consistent with international practice and confidence-building. It also calls for universal adherence to the NPT worldwide.
entries.quote: aimed at building trust and addressing the political and security obstacles that have impeded progress. Such efforts must be pursued voluntarily on the basis of arrangements mutually agreed upon by all the regional states, consistent with international practices. The United States looks forward to the day when the NPT is truly universal, not just in the Middle East but everywhere.

entries.position_summary: The country emphasizes transparency by inviting ambassadors and NGO experts to visit current and former nuclear weapons facilities, aiming to increase openness and build confidence. These visits serve as confidence-building measures to reduce mistrust regarding its nuclear activities.
entries.quote: That is also why, a few weeks ago, we hosted ambassadors from around the world and nongovernmental experts for visits to current and former nuclear weapons facilities.

entries.position_summary: The United States signals readiness to engage in bilateral arms control discussions with Russia and China without preconditions. This reflects openness to dialogue as a means of advancing arms control and reducing strategic risk.
entries.quote: Mr. Chair, All this notwithstanding, our National Security Advisor has made clear that the United States is ready to engage in bilateral arms control discussions with Russia and with the PRC, without preconditions.

entries.position_summary: The country advocates for engaging in arms control dialogues with China and Russia to reduce the risk of nuclear conflict and urges States Parties to support these efforts. It dismisses alternative proposals as empty and unverifiable, implicitly criticizing the TPNW.
entries.quote: By contrast, President Trump has made clear his readiness to have arms control dialogues with China and Russia, with the goal of reducing the risks of nuclear conflict. We hope that States Parties will focus their energy on calling on them to do so. Join us in that dialogue instead of echoing empty and unverifiable proposals.

entries.position_summary: The country urges the PRC to engage in substantive strategic nuclear dialogue to prevent miscalculation and miscommunication. It signals support for risk-reduction, transparency, and potential arms control engagement with China.
entries.quote: It is time for the PRC substantively to engage with us on strategic nuclear issues in order to avoid risks of miscalculation and miscommunication.
